title: Collective decay of interacting bosons
authors: Bennet Windt, Lorenzo Rossi, Alexander V. Poshakinskiy, Daniel Malz, Dominik S. Wild
abstract: We study a bosonic analog of the paradigmatic Dicke model of superradiance, comprising interacting bosonic modes subject to fully symmetric collective decay. Depending on the interaction strength, we uncover qualitatively distinct regimes of emission. For strong interactions, the emission closely resembles Dicke superradiance, with perturbative corrections arising from the presence of additional levels. For weaker interactions, the bosonic statistics qualitatively changes the dynamics, leading to a crossover to subradiant emission. Remarkably, we show that the dynamics in this regime can be described by rate equations analogous to those of the Dicke model despite the large accessible bosonic Hilbert space. Our findings are based on a combination of analytical arguments and large-scale numerics enabled by the permutational symmetry of the problem and may be probed in circuit QED experiments.
summary: The paper explores the collective decay of interacting bosons, modeling it as an analog to the Dicke model. It identifies distinct physical regimes based on interaction strength, showing crossovers from superradiant to subradiant emission. The methodology successfully reduces complex many-body dynamics into manageable rate equations, providing deep insights applicable to circuit QED experiments.
main_problem: The study investigates the collective decay dynamics of interacting bosonic modes, serving as a bosonic analog to the Dicke model of superradiance.
main_result: Distinct regimes are found based on interaction strength: strong interactions yield Dicke-like superradiance, while weak interactions cause a crossover to subradiant emission that surprisingly retains an analogy to rate equations.
method: The analysis combines analytical arguments (e.g., adiabatic elimination, variational methods) with large-scale numerical simulations leveraging the problem's permutational symmetry.
model_or_system: The system consists of $N$ bosonic resonators interacting via a repulsive on-site interaction ($U$) and undergoing collective decay governed by a Lindblad master equation. The dynamics are analyzed in various limits, including non-interacting ($U=0$), large-$U$, and small-$U$.
key_observables: Collective emission rate $R(t)$, peak emission rate $R_{pk}$, and the scaling exponent $ eta$ of $R_{pk}$ with system size $N$.
important_parameters:
Interaction strength ($U$), decay rate ($\gamma$), system size ($N$), and excitation density ($
u$). Key regimes are defined by the ratio $U/(\gamma N)$.
main_assumptions: The analysis frequently restricts itself to the permutation-invariant subspace, and in some limits (small-$U$), it relies on adiabatic elimination or approximations based on the quantum de Finetti theorem.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate emission rate dynamics for varying interaction strengths, plot scaling exponents ($ eta$ vs $\alpha$), and visualize the structure of the effective dark subspace states and associated rate equations.
paper_structure: The paper systematically analyzes the decay in different coupling regimes ($U \gg N\gamma$, $U \ll N\gamma$) by mapping the full dynamics onto simplified, yet physically accurate, sets of coupled rate equations or restricted subspaces.
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed theoretical understanding of how bosonic statistics modify fundamental quantum phenomena like superradiance, offering predictive scaling laws ($ eta = 2\alpha$ vs $ eta=2$) applicable to cavity QED implementations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Bosonic collective decay. (a) Emission rate dynamics for N = 10 resonators subject to varied interaction strengths U. The limiting cases of two-level emitters (U →∞) and free bosons (U = 0) are indicated by black dashed lines. (b) Regimes of collective emission for a tunable non-linearity U ∼γN α, characterized by distinct asymptotic scalings of the peak emission rate Rpk ∼γN β. We plot the relation between the scaling exponents α and β as given by our theoretical pre- diction (solid line) and extracted from permutation-invariant numerical simulations (markers).

FIG. 2. Large-U dynamics. (a) Schematic illustration of the auxiliary lattice model, Eq. (3). The action of the Hamiltonian and the collective jump operator are indicated graphically. (b) Graphical representation of the action of the diagonal dissipator D∞and the leading-order large-U perturbative correction δD on the auxiliary lattice. (c) Comparison of the exact and perturbative emission dynamics for N = 10 and two large values of U. The Dicke solution R∞(t) is shown for reference.

FIG. 3. Small-U dynamics. (a) Comparison of the exact (solid) and effective (dashed) emission dynamics for N = 30 and varied U in the small-U regime. The analytical values of the emission rate plateaus Rd(0) [41] are indicated by horizontal lines and the emission rate R0(t) for U = 0 is also included for reference. (b) Transition rates R(1) n and (c) R(2) n in the ground state manifold of the effective Hamiltonian Hd (blue markers), for system sizes N = 10, 20, . . . , 100 (indicated by shading). The de Finetti predictions obtained in the End Matter (along with the associated critical excitation density νc = 1 −1/ √

FIG. 4. Multi-peak dynamics. Dynamics of multi-excited initial Fock states with M0 = 2 (left) and M0 = 3 (right) initial excitations per mode, for N = 100 modes. The emission burst peaks are indicated by vertical dashed lines.
